short.headline: India's air today depends heavily on the city you check
short.dek: The latest WAQI station snapshots show Mumbai highest among these five cities, followed by Delhi, Chennai, Kolkata, and Bengaluru.
short.body: The latest WAQI snapshots do not give one national air-quality number. They give city station readings. In this batch, Mumbai is highest at AQI 156, Delhi is 95, Chennai 73, Kolkata 65, and Bengaluru 58. PM2.5 is the largest shown component in each city snapshot. Read this like a weather check, not like an annual health verdict. The stations are useful because they are current and concrete, but they do not represent every neighbourhood in India. For a fair “India air” story, use several city snapshots and keep the timestamp visible.

article.title: How bad is India's air today?
article.standfirst: There is no single “India air today” number. The honest answer is a city snapshot, with timestamps and station names attached.
article.bodyMarkdown:
## What is the headline answer?

Today's air story is not one India number. It is a set of city station snapshots. In the latest WAQI data on this page, Mumbai is highest at AQI 156. Delhi is 95. Chennai is 73, Kolkata 65, and Bengaluru 58.

That order matters, but the timestamp matters too. Delhi's reading is from 2026-06-01 09:00 IST. The other city readings shown here are from 2026-05-30. Air changes by hour, weather, wind, traffic, fires, dust, and local activity. Treat the chart like a current reading, not a permanent label.

## What exactly is AQI measuring?

AQI is an index. It compresses pollutant readings into a simpler air-quality number. That makes it easy to compare cities quickly, but it also hides detail. Two places can have similar AQI numbers for different pollutant mixes.

That is why the page shows components like PM2.5, PM10, ozone, and nitrogen dioxide where WAQI provides them. In these five snapshots, PM2.5 is the biggest shown component in each city. PM2.5 means very fine particles. The chart is telling you that the small-particle reading is carrying much of the visible AQI story here.

## Why show several cities instead of only Delhi?

Delhi air matters, but India is not Delhi. A Delhi-only page would be easier, and also misleading. Mumbai, Kolkata, Chennai, and Bengaluru give the reader a wider first look. The point is not to crown one city forever. The point is to avoid pretending one station can speak for the whole country.

This is also why the visual should be simple on mobile: one city per bar, one latest AQI value, and a timestamp nearby. Readers should be able to see the ranking without squinting.

## How should a normal reader use this page?

Use it the way you use a weather app. First ask: where is the station? Then ask: when was it updated? Then ask: which pollutant is driving the number? If the answer is old, local, or missing components, lower your confidence.

For daily life, the city snapshot is useful. It can tell you whether today looks worse in one place than another. But it cannot tell you your exact street exposure, your indoor exposure, or the long-term yearly average for India.

## What does this page not tell us?

It does not tell us annual PM2.5 exposure for India. It does not tell us how many people are exposed to each AQI level. It does not tell us the health burden of air pollution. It also does not compare rural and urban air.

Those are important questions, but they need different datasets. This page is deliberately narrower: a latest city snapshot from WAQI, shown plainly and not oversold.

## What should the reader remember?

Air quality is local and time-sensitive. A single national sentence can become fake very quickly. The better habit is to read city, station, timestamp, AQI, and pollutant components together.

For these latest snapshots, Mumbai is the highest among the five cities shown. But the bigger lesson is the method: do not ask “how is India's air?” without also asking “where, when, and measured by whom?”

editorialPlan.heroDescription: A latest city-snapshot page for air quality, with station and timestamp discipline.

selectedDataPoints.label: Mumbai air quality index: aqi
selectedDataPoints.reason: Highest latest AQI among the five city snapshots.

selectedDataPoints.label: Delhi air quality index: aqi
selectedDataPoints.reason: Important comparison city, but not the only India signal.

pullQuotes.quote: There is no honest one-number answer for India's air today.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: AQI is an index that turns pollutant readings into one easier number. It is useful for quick comparison, but it hides detail.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: The page compares city snapshots by AQI.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: PM2.5 means very fine particles in the air. These particles are small enough to be a serious air-quality concern.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: PM2.5 is the largest shown component in all five city snapshots.

glossaryBlocks.plainMeaning: A snapshot is a reading from a place at a time. It is not a full-year average and not a reading for every neighbourhood.
glossaryBlocks.whyItMattersHere: WAQI readings are useful only when the city, station, and time are kept visible.

chartExplainers.title: Delhi air quality index
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Delhi is an important city snapshot, but not the whole India answer.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Read AQI with the station name and timestamp.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not generalise one Delhi station to all of India.

chartExplainers.title: Mumbai air quality index
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Mumbai is highest among the five snapshots here.
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare AQI first, then pollutant components.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not treat a snapshot as a yearly average.

chartExplainers.title: Kolkata air quality index
chartExplainers.howToRead: Use it as one station snapshot.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not compare readings without checking timestamps.

chartExplainers.title: Chennai air quality index
chartExplainers.howToRead: Compare it with the other city snapshots.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not assume the reading applies to every Chennai neighbourhood.

chartExplainers.title: Bengaluru air quality index
chartExplainers.whyShowThis: Bengaluru adds another southern city snapshot.
chartExplainers.mistakeToAvoid: Do not make a national claim from one city.

sourceNotes: Latest city air-quality snapshots are from WAQI local station feeds.
caveats: WAQI readings are station snapshots, not national averages.
Cities have different update times in this batch, so compare with timestamp caution.
This page does not show annual PM2.5 exposure or health burden.

What comparison would stop the number from being misread?

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.

dataSelectionAudit.areSelectedDataPointsGood: yes, five city snapshots answer the daily/latest question better than a Delhi-only page
addDataPoints: National annual PM2.5 exposure and population-weighted exposure would improve the long-term health story.
removeDataPoints: CO2 emissions should not be used here; air quality and climate emissions are different questions.

visualizationDecisions.reason: City bars are the fastest mobile comparison.
Components explain what is behind the AQI number without a dense table.
